functional_unit.notes: All data is normalized to 1 brick of the final product.

clay_brick.source: Yüksek & Öztaş (2020) 'The evaluation of fired clay brick production in terms of energy efficiency: a case study in Turkey'
clay_brick.notes: Standard fired clay brick with dimensions 19×19×13.5 cm. Represents typical masonry unit used in East African construction.

pet_brick.source: Estimated at 80% of clay brick weight (20% reduction in density). Based on Das & Das (2017) and general plastic vs. clay density comparisons.
pet_brick.notes: Plastic density (1.2-1.4 g/cm³) is lighter than fired clay (1.8+ g/cm³), resulting in approximately 20% weight reduction while maintaining similar dimensions.

climate_change.source: Gweilo et al. (2011) adapted for Kenyan context
climate_change.notes: Emissions from diesel-powered excavation of clay.

water_depletion.notes: Water use in clay extraction is considered negligible.

climate_change.notes: Electricity for crushing, grinding, and screening.

land_use.notes: Processing occurs at the manufacturing plant site.

climate_change.source: Prateep Na Talang et al. (2017); Koroneos & Dompros (2007)
climate_change.notes: Based on typical clamp kiln emissions using biomass/coal. Prateep et al. studied similar traditional kilns in Thailand. Koroneos et al. quantified Greek brick production. Value adjusted for developing country context with less efficient kilns.

energy_demand.notes: Thermal energy for firing dominates. Koroneos et al. reported 4.0-5.0 MJ/kg for industrial kilns; López-Aguilar reported up to 5.5 MJ/kg for traditional regional manufacture. Value 4.5 MJ/kg represents mid-range for clamp/traditional kilns.

water_depletion.notes: Water for clay preparation and mixing. Mohajerani et al. (2018) 'Fired-clay bricks incorporating biosolids' in Journal of Materials in Civil Engineering reported 1.2-2.0 L/kg for water mixing. Koroneos et al. (2007) reported similar ranges. Value 1.5 L/kg is mid-range.

particulate_matter.source: Rajarathnam et al. (2014); López-Aguilar et al. (2016)
particulate_matter.notes: Major emissions from inefficient kiln firing. Rajarathnam et al. (2014) 'Assessment of air pollutant emissions from brick kilns' in Atmospheric Environment found 0.6-1.2 g PM2.5/kg for traditional clamp kilns. López-Aguilar reported similar range. Value 0.8 g PM2.5/kg reflects typical emissions from developing-country brick production.

climate_change.notes: Assumes 50 km transport distance by a medium-duty truck.
Impact of cement mortar production, assuming 0.15 kg mortar per kg of brick.

energy_demand.notes: Energy for cement production in mortar.

particulate_matter.notes: Dust and emissions from cement production.

land_use.notes: Land use for quarrying cement raw materials.

climate_change.source: Shen et al. (2010); Lee et al. (2017)
climate_change.notes: Avoided burden: methane emissions avoided from landfill disposal. Shen et al. (2010) quantified PET recycling benefits over virgin production. Lee et al. (2017) in Journal of Cleaner Production quantified landfill methane emissions at 40-60 kg CO2e per ton of plastic waste, translating to -2.0 to -3.0 kg CO2e per kg recycled. Value -2.5 is mid-range conservative estimate.

energy_demand.notes: Energy for manual collection and transport of waste plastic.

water_depletion.notes: Water use in collection is negligible.

particulate_matter.source: Zhao et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2024)
particulate_matter.notes: Avoided burden: particulate emissions avoided from open burning or uncontrolled landfill decomposition. Zhao et al. (2019) Columbia University study "Methane emissions from landfills" estimated PM2.5 from uncontrolled waste sites. Wang et al. (2024) Nature Sustainability: "Methane emissions from landfills differentially underestimated worldwide" provides updated estimates. Value -1.5 g PM2.5/kg reflects avoided combustion and decomposition-related particulates.

climate_change.notes: Electricity for sorting, washing, and shredding PET.

water_depletion.notes: Water for washing shredded plastic flakes.

land_use.notes: Processing occurs at the manufacturing plant site.

climate_change.source: Shen et al. (2010); Kousemaker et al. (2021)
climate_change.notes: Electricity for extrusion/compression molding. Shen et al. (2010) 'Open-loop recycling: A LCA case study of PET bottle-to-fibre recycling' quantified grid electricity impacts. Kousemaker et al. (2021) in Applied Sciences provides critical review of plastic recycling LCA, confirming 0.06-0.10 kg CO2e/kg for processing electricity in developed-world contexts. Value 0.08 kg CO2e/kg reflects electricity consumption.

energy_demand.source: Civancik-Uslu et al. (2021); Shen et al. (2010)
energy_demand.notes: Electrical energy for heating and molding. Civancik-Uslu et al. (2021) 'Moving from linear to circular household plastic packaging' in Resources, Conservation and Recycling: 1.0-1.4 MJ/kg for mechanical extrusion/molding. No water-intensive firing process. Value 1.2 MJ/kg is typical for thermochemical recycling processing.

water_depletion.notes: No water used in the molding process.

climate_change.notes: Assumes 50 km transport distance by a medium-duty truck.
